text: Three 8mm trocars are positioned for the robotic umbilical hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure using the OptiView technique with a robotic eight millimeter trocar, inserting it into the surgical field.

After obtaining access to the peritoneum, the surgeon achieves insufflation to fifteen millimeters of mercury. The video shows a small umbilical defect with a portion of yellow fat visibly incarcerated within it.
The surgeon places additional trocars under direct visualization, ensuring precise positioning within the surgical field.
With the trocars in place, the surgical team prepares to dock the robotic system, ensuring precise alignment and setup for the procedure.
A small hernia is visualized, with a portion of fat appearing to be incarcerated within the defect.
The surgeon uses scissors equipped with electrocautery and a pro grasp instrument, likely for precise cutting and manipulation of tissue.
The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap approximately ten centimeters in diameter to ensure ample space for the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection using scissors, carefully navigating a fine plane above the peritoneum to preserve muscle fibers and minimize tissue injury.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection near the umbilical hernia sac, carefully pushing away tissue to avoid penetrating the sac, ensuring a smooth peritoneal covering over the mesh.
The surgeon demonstrates maintaining dissection within the correct anatomical plane, resulting in minimal bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon performs sweeping motions with minimal use of electrocautery to maintain a bloodless surgical field, carefully avoiding contamination.
The surgeon observes the defect opening up, revealing the underlying anatomical structures as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon carefully removes the umbilical hernia sac to minimize the sensation of a foreign body after closure, ensuring a smooth peritoneal covering over the mesh.
The surgeon separates the skin from the underlying hernia sac, revealing the distinct layers between the two structures.
The surgeon exposes a clear defect in the fascia at the umbilicus, visible through the robotic 3D optics.
The hernia defect is clearly identified, measuring approximately two centimeters. The surgeon opens a nine-centimeter mesh to cover the defect.

The robotic system provides a clear view of the anatomy. The surgeon uses a ProGrasp instrument, with tips approximately two centimeters in size, to measure. Sutures are being passed through the tissue, with the intention of closing the peritoneum before placing the mesh.
The surgeon places a suture slightly away from the umbilical fascia defect, locking it in place. The suture appears robust, allowing for significant tension to effectively close the defect.

The surgeon uses a PDS suture to close the hernia defect with two passes, tightening the suture as throws are made. The first layer is completed, and the surgeon prepares to start the second layer, moving back towards the initial suture entry point for additional reinforcement and tissue approximation.

The surgeon confirms the complete closure of the defect, with the assistant palpating to expel any trapped air and ensure no lipoma remains. The coated mesh is then unrolled in preparation for placement.

The surgeon places the coated mesh towards the bowel, ensuring there is no direct contact between the mesh and intra-abdominal organs or the small bowel. An absorbable two-arm suture is used to secure the mesh, avoiding the need for laparoscopic tacks.
The surgeon uses a plier to suture the mesh in place, securing it on one side with a double-arm suture to provide fixation and avoid the use of a laparoscopic tacker.
The surgeon sutures the remaining portion of the mesh in place, ensuring it is securely fixed to prevent future migration.
The surgeon uses a CT-one needle for fascial closure, selecting between twelve or eighteen-inch options provided by Stratafix.
The surgeon uses a 24 by 24 centimeter bidirectional suture to secure the mesh in place.

The surgeon fixes the mesh in place by re-approximating the peritoneum, ensuring separation from the small bowel to reduce future adhesions. The mesh is sandwiched between the peritoneum, and the surgical site is being closed.
The surgeon finalizes the closure of the peritoneum, securing the mesh in place. The sutures are tied down and subsequently removed.
A small piece of a four by four gauze is placed over the surgical site, followed by the application of a transparent Tegaderm dressing to secure it.
The surgeon inserts a needle into the surgical site.
The surgeon connects the suction device to the needle, creating a tight vacuum seal in the surgical area.
